name: PyOpenCL (OpenCL GPU Computing)

auth.notes: Local library; requires OpenCL runtime (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel GPU/CPU)

performance.notes: Depends on GPU hardware; cross-vendor GPU computing

agent_readiness.notes: OpenCL GPGPU computing for AMD/Intel/NVIDIA GPUs. import pyopencl as cl. import pyopencl.array as cla. ctx = cl.create_some_context(). queue = cl.CommandQueue(ctx). program_src = '__kernel void add(__global float *a, __global float *b, __global float *c) { int i = get_global_id(0); c[i] = a[i] + b[i]; }'. prg = cl.Program(ctx, program_src).build(). a_np = np.random.rand(50000).astype(np.float32). a_g = cl.Buffer(ctx, cl.mem_flags.COPY_HOST_PTR, hostbuf=a_np). High-level: a_dev = cla.to_device(queue, a_np). result = a_dev + b_dev. Elementwise kernels. Reduction. clblas/clFFT. Works on any OpenCL-capable device.

gotchas: OpenCL kernel code in C-like language — different from Python
Device selection: multiple platforms/devices can cause confusion
NVIDIA users often prefer CUDA/CuPy for better performance
Cross-vendor compatibility advantage is also complexity cost
No type annotations; verbose low-level API
best_when: GPGPU computing on AMD or Intel GPUs where CUDA is unavailable
Cross-platform GPU code targeting multiple vendors
When running on Apple Silicon (Metal OpenCL) or CPU OpenCL
avoid_when: NVIDIA GPUs (use CuPy or PyTorch CUDA for better performance)
ML training (use PyTorch/JAX with native GPU backends)
summary: PyOpenCL provides OpenCL GPGPU computing across AMD/Intel/NVIDIA/CPU. cl.create_some_context(). cl.Program(ctx, kernel_src).build(). cl.Buffer + kernel execution. High-level array: cla.to_device(). Elementwise, reduction. MIT by Andreas Klöckner/UIUC. Best for cross-vendor GPU computing without CUDA. NVIDIA users prefer CuPy. OpenCL kernel C-code required. Verbose low-level API.
